teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the everyday question of whether the system knows what actually happened.
teaching_arc.progression: Define the two control styles, compare them, and then connect them to robotics consequences.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish with a practical test for deciding whether feedback matters in a given system.

audience_level: undergraduate industrial robotics students preparing for labs, robot selection, and basic programming decisions
topic_goal: Build a strong intuition for why feedback changes control quality and when open-loop control stops being trustworthy.
topic_takeaways: Students should distinguish open-loop and closed-loop control clearly.
Students should understand why disturbance rejection and correction require feedback.
Students should be able to explain when open-loop control is acceptable and when it is risky.
style_notes: Keep the tone practical and shop-floor oriented.
Translate definitions into robot-cell decisions quickly.
Prefer concrete industrial examples over abstract theory when possible.
Keep returning to the question of disturbance and correction.
Use simple examples before formal robotics wording.
transition_style: Move from the industrial question students would ask on the job to the next specification, control, or programming choice they need to make.

slides.narration_seed: Welcome students and set expectations: this topic is control basics. We’ll connect it directly to robotics: robot servos are closed-loop; some timed processes are open-loop.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Build a strong intuition for why feedback changes control quality and when open-loop control stops being trustworthy.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Industrial Robots are Control Systems so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may memorize ROS 2 terms without seeing the actual data-flow relationship between them.
slides.explanation_style: Frame Control Systems as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep Control Systems connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Control Systems sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Original slide had an automotive robot-line photo. Tell students: robots in factories rely on closed-loop servo control.
must_cover: In industry, accuracy and repeatability usually require feedback.
Many everyday machines are open-loop because they are simpler and cheaper.
Today: define both systems, study diagrams, and compare advantages / disadvantages.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Control Systems into Industrial Robots are Control Systems so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into OPEN AND CLOSED LOOP so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Industrial Robots are Control Systems to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Industrial Robots are Control Systems connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Industrial Robots are Control Systems sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Explain: we simplify reality with block diagrams. Tell them we’ll use boxes/arrows all semester.
must_cover: Control systems are increasingly important in modern civilization and technology.
Even if a real system is complex, we can model it as:
• Input (objective) → Control system → Output (result)
Many day-to-day activities are affected by some type of control system.
must_say: Closed-loop control can correct itself because it measures what actually happened.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Industrial Robots are Control Systems into OPEN AND CLOSED LOOP so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into What you can expect from this course so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often describe feedback correctly in words but still miss what disturbances do to open-loop behavior.
slides.explanation_style: Use OPEN AND CLOSED LOOP to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep OPEN AND CLOSED LOOP connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how OPEN AND CLOSED LOOP sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slide_ref.source_title: What you can expect from this course

slides.narration_seed: Explain: this is the tree. Everything today is under open-loop or closed-loop.
must_cover: Open-loop systems and Closed-loop systems
This is the high-level classification we will use today.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into OPEN LOOP SYSTEMS so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for What you can expect from this course first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep What you can expect from this course connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture What you can expect from this course clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Point to the diagram: r(t) → controller → plant → c(t). Emphasize: **NO feedback arrow** returning.
must_cover: A control system that cannot adjust itself to changes is an open-loop control system.
In general, many manual control systems behave like open-loop systems.
The block diagram of an open-loop control system is shown here.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Open-loop: Signals and Meaning so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for OPEN LOOP SYSTEMS first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep OPEN LOOP SYSTEMS connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture OPEN LOOP SYSTEMS clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Explain slowly: If something goes wrong, the system does not correct itself. A human must change the input.
must_cover: r(t) = input (reference) signal.
u(t) = control / actuating signal (what controller sends to plant).
c(t) = output signal.
For a constant input, the output remains unaltered (no automatic correction).
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Open-loop: Signals and Meaning to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Open-loop: Signals and Meaning is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Open-loop: Signals and Meaning in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Open-loop: Signals and Meaning connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Open-loop: Signals and Meaning accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF OPEN LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM

slides.narration_seed: Turn each example into a quick discussion: ‘What is missing?’ **Feedback**. Dryer does not measure dryness; washer doesn’t measure cleanliness; toaster doesn’t measure toast color.
must_cover: Electric Hand Drier: hot air comes out regardless of how dry your hand is.
Automatic Washing Machine: runs for preset time regardless of whether washing is complete.
Bread Toaster: runs for adjusted time regardless of whether toasting is complete.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF OPEN LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF OPEN LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF OPEN LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF OPEN LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF OPEN LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF OPEN LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM

slides.narration_seed: Ask students: which of these could be upgraded into closed-loop? Example: dryer with humidity sensor; light with brightness sensor; stereo with automatic level control (in some systems).
must_cover: Automatic Tea/Coffee Maker: functions for pre-adjusted time only.
Timer Based Clothes Drier: dries for pre-adjusted time regardless of dryness level.
Light Switch: lamp glows whenever switch is on regardless of whether light is required.
Stereo Volume: adjusted manually regardless of output volume level.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF OPEN LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF OPEN LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF OPEN LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF OPEN LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF OPEN LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Underline the tradeoff: simplicity vs accuracy. This sets up closed-loop: it improves accuracy but costs complexity.
must_cover: a) Simple in construction and design
b) Economical
c) Easy to maintain
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
d) Generally stable
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF OPEN LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Open-loop: Advantages vs Disadvantages explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Open-loop: Advantages vs Disadvantages and know which side fits which situation.

slides.narration_seed: Don’t rush this slide. Explain the ‘error’ idea: desired - actual.
must_cover: A system that can respond to changes and make corrections by itself is a closed-loop control system.
Main difference vs open-loop: presence of feedback action.
Signals (as used in the original explanation):
r(t) = input signal (reference).
slides.transition_from_previous: Use CLOSED LOOP SYSTEMS to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once CLOSED LOOP SYSTEMS is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define CLOSED LOOP SYSTEMS in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep CLOSED LOOP SYSTEMS connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain CLOSED LOOP SYSTEMS accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Walk through the loop: r(t) enters comparator; comparator outputs e(t); controller produces u(t); plant produces c(t); feedback produces b(t); b(t) goes back to comparator. Then ask: ‘Where is the decision-making happening?’ At the controller based on error.
must_cover: Closed-loop differs from open-loop by the presence of feedback.
Key signals:
• r(t) reference input
• e(t) error signal
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF CLOSED LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slide_ref.source_title: PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF CLOSED LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM

slides.narration_seed: For each example, ask: what is the sensor/measurement? Iron: thermostat; stabilizer: voltage measurement; tank: level sensor; AC: thermostat; car cooling: temperature sensor.
must_cover: 1) Automatic Electric Iron: heating controlled by output temperature of the iron.
2) Servo Voltage Stabilizer: operates depending upon output voltage.
3) Water Level Controller: input water controlled by reservoir water level.
4) Missile + Radar Tracking: direction controlled by comparing target and missile position.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF CLOSED LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF CLOSED LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF CLOSED LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF CLOSED LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain PRACTICAL EXAMPLES OF CLOSED LOOP CONTROL SYSTEM accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Advantages of CLOSED LOOP Control System

slides.narration_seed: Explain in plain terms: accuracy improves because errors get corrected. Automation becomes possible because the system can self-correct.
must_cover: a) More accurate even in the presence of non-linearity.
b) Errors are corrected due to feedback → high accuracy. Bandwidth range is large.
c) Facilitates automation.
d) Sensitivity can be made small to make system more stable.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Advantages of CLOSED LOOP Control System to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Advantages of CLOSED LOOP Control System is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Advantages of CLOSED LOOP Control System in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Advantages of CLOSED LOOP Control System connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Advantages of CLOSED LOOP Control System accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Disadvantages of CLOSED LOOP Control System

slides.narration_seed: Key teaching: feedback is powerful but can cause oscillations if poorly designed. Relate to a thermostat that turns on/off too aggressively causing temperature swings (simple intuition).
must_cover: a) Costlier
b) More complicated to design
c) Requires more maintenance
d) Feedback can lead to oscillatory response
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Disadvantages of CLOSED LOOP Control System to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Disadvantages of CLOSED LOOP Control System is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Disadvantages of CLOSED LOOP Control System in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Disadvantages of CLOSED LOOP Control System connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Disadvantages of CLOSED LOOP Control System accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Tell them: this is the tradeoff summary slide. If they remember only one thing: open-loop is simple/cheap but inaccurate; closed-loop is accurate/robust but complex and must be designed for stability.
must_cover: 1. Inaccurate
2. Unreliable
3. Stable
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
4. Bandwidth is small
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Disadvantages of CLOSED LOOP Control System into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Open-loop vs Closed-loop (Comparison) explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Open-loop vs Closed-loop (Comparison) and know which side fits which situation.

slides.narration_seed: After they answer, point to the closed-loop diagram: show the feedback arrow. Then connect to robots: encoders measure position continuously (feedback).
must_cover: Which feature must exist for a system to be called closed-loop?
Closed-loop control requires feedback: output is measured and compared to the reference to form an error signal.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
must_say: Closed-loop control can correct itself because it measures what actually happened.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: Feedback and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students often describe feedback correctly in words but still miss what disturbances do to open-loop behavior.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: Feedback into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: Feedback question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slides.narration_seed: Ask follow-up: what sensor would make it closed-loop? Answer: color sensor / temperature sensor / smoke sensor, etc.
must_cover: A bread toaster runs for a fixed time and stops, regardless of the toast color. What type of control is this?
This is open-loop because the toaster does not measure the output (toast color) and does not correct itself.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: Open-loop or Closed-loop? and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Quiz: Open-loop or Closed-loop? without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: Open-loop or Closed-loop? into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: Open-loop or Closed-loop? question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slides.narration_seed: Bridge to next topics: servo motors + encoders + controller = closed-loop. This is why robots can repeat positions accurately.
must_cover: Industrial robots typically use encoders to measure joint position and correct motion in real time. This is an example of:
Encoders provide feedback, so the controller can correct errors continuously → closed-loop.
Students should distinguish open-loop and closed-loop control clearly.
Students should understand why disturbance rejection and correction require feedback.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Quiz: Robots to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Questions? so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Quiz: Robots as a final self-check so students leave knowing what they should now be able to explain or do.
slides.story_hint: Keep Quiz: Robots connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave Quiz: Robots with the main takeaways of the topic compressed into usable memory.

slides.narration_seed: Open the floor. If quiet, ask: ‘Give me one open-loop example from your daily life’ and ‘Give me one closed-loop example.’
must_cover: Ask about anything unclear: signals, diagrams, examples, or the comparison.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Questions? to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
